key_departures.impact: Pro Bowl center retired unexpectedly at age 27, creating an immediate void at the most important interior line position. Bears traded a 2027 5th-round pick to New England for Garrett Bradbury as a direct response.

Bears traded Moore and a 2026 5th-round pick (No. 164) to Buffalo for a 2026 2nd-round pick (No. 60). Moore was their WR1 and a $114M extension player. His departure creates WR depth/starter need alongside Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III.

Released by Bears to clear ~$15M in cap space. Edmunds led Chicago in tackles (112 in 2025) despite missing 4 games. Signed a 3yr/$36M deal with the Giants. Significant starter-level loss at LB, though replaced partially by Devin Bush signing and D'Marco Jackson re-sign.

Led the NFL with 7 interceptions in 2025. Signed a 1yr/$9M deal with New England. Bears had pre-signed Coby Bryant before Byard's departure, but losing a Pro Bowl safety still leaves the S2 role unsettled.

Signed a 1yr/$5.5M deal with Pittsburgh. A Bears 2nd-round pick in 2022, Brisker was a multi-year starting safety. Departure alongside Byard and Gardner-Johnson leaves the S2 opposite Bryant completely open.
Signed a 1yr/$6M deal with Buffalo. Gardner-Johnson was a versatile mid-season acquisition in 2025 who played 10 games and provided slot/safety hybrid value. His departure deepens the secondary hole.

Signed a 1yr/$5.5M deal with the Jets. Wright had a breakout year with 5 interceptions, 11 PDs and Pro Bowl honors. His departure creates a CB2/3 need that was not previously critical given his emergence.

Signed a 1yr/$4M deal with Houston. Rotational edge rusher who developed from a 5th-round project into a solid depth piece. Loss thins a pass rush group that was already ranked 31st in pass-rush win rate.

Started 14-of-17 games in 2025 as the nose tackle. Bears allowed him to leave in free agency as part of a youth movement. His departure leaves a run-stuffing gap at DT alongside Grady Jarrett and Gervon Dexter.
Expected to return to Atlanta. Was WR3/slot contributor. Loss compounds Moore trade departure at WR depth.
Signed a 1yr/$2M deal with Atlanta. Depth DT loss, minimal roster impact.
Signed a 1-year deal with Indianapolis. Reserve safety and special teams contributor. Low impact departure.
Released by Bears to clear ~$2M in cap space. Core special teams contributor but minimal defensive impact.

signings.analysis: 3yr/$40M with $25.75M guaranteed — a real investment in a young, ascending safety who just won a Super Bowl. Bryant fills one of the two starting safety holes left by the departures of Byard, Brisker, and Gardner-Johnson. He is the clear S1 going forward. However, the S2 position remains unsettled, making this only a partial address of the safety need. Bears still need to find a starter-quality partner opposite Bryant. Need drops from critical (tier 1) to a moderate (tier 2) hole.

3yr/$30M with $21M guaranteed is a legitimate starter investment. Bush re-emerged in 2025 with 125 tackles, 3 INTs, 2 forced fumbles and 2 sacks in 17 starts — the only player with multiple pick-sixes. He replaces Tremaine Edmunds as the starting LB next to T.J. Edwards, though Edwards is recovering from a fractured fibula and Noah Sewell has a torn Achilles. LB remains thin in depth, partially addressed at the starter level. Need drops from tier 1 (critical post-Edmunds release) to tier 2.

2yr/$12M with $5M guaranteed is a rotational-tier investment. Gallimore had a career-high 3.5 sacks and 38 tackles in 2025 and profiles as a replacement for Andrew Billings at nose tackle. He has only 26 career starts in 83 games — clearly a rotation piece, not a true starter upgrade. The Bears entered FA with only Grady Jarrett and Gervon Dexter on the DT roster. Gallimore adds a third body but doesn't solve the need for a high-quality starting DT. IDL remains a draft priority.

signings.notes: Acquired via trade (2027 5th-round pick to NE). Cap hit of $5.7M.
signings.analysis: Trade acquisition to replace the retired Drew Dalman at center. Bradbury is a known Ben Johnson commodity (worked together in Minnesota/Detroit) and is a proven starting center who will stabilize the position in 2026. However, this is a 1-year bridge at $5.7M against the cap — no long-term security at center. The Bears are likely to draft a center in 2026 to develop behind Bradbury. Need shifts from critical (Dalman retirement) to moderate — Bradbury is a solid band-aid but not a multi-year solution.

1yr/up to $5.1M is a depth/return-specialist signing. Raymond reunites with Ben Johnson, who knows him well from Detroit. He replaces Devin Duvernay's return specialist role and provides WR4 depth. Rome Odunze, Luther Burden III, and Jahdae Walker form the top-3 receiver group. Raymond does not address the WR2 void left by trading DJ Moore — WR remains a significant draft need.

2yr/$6M is below-starter money for CB. Lewis spent 6 years in Buffalo primarily as a reserve with 10 starts over the last two seasons. He replaces Nahshon Wright in the CB depth chart but is not the starting CB2 solution. Jaylon Johnson remains locked in at CB1. CB depth has been addressed minimally; a starter-quality CB2/nickel option is still needed — likely via the draft.

1-year depth signing at below-market value. Street collected 4 sacks over 676 snaps across 2.5 seasons in Atlanta. This is essentially the Dominique Robinson replacement — a rotational lineman who can play multiple D-line spots and contribute on special teams. Does nothing to address the Bears' critical EDGE starter need alongside Montez Sweat. EDGE remains the biggest unaddressed need heading into the draft.

1-year low-risk deal for a former top-10 pick who missed all of 2025 rehabbing a complex knee/femur procedure. Wills will compete with Braxton Jones, Theo Benedet, and Kiran Amegadjie for the LT job while Ozzy Trapilo recovers. High upside if healthy but cannot be counted on as the starter — treat as a lottery ticket. OT remains a draft priority given Trapilo's patellar tendon injury expected to sideline him most of 2026.

Re-signed on a 1yr/$5M deal. Jones started 44 games for the Bears over 4 seasons but was inconsistent in 2025 before a knee injury cost him much of the season. He enters another LT competition with Wills, Benedet, and Amegadjie. This is a cost-effective bridge that maintains the competition, but does not upgrade the position or solve the long-term OT need. OT is still a top-3 draft target.

Re-signed on 2yr/$7.5M, max $10.5M. Jackson stepped up well in 2025 when Edmunds and Edwards were injured, earning two NFC Defensive Player of the Week awards. He now projects as a starting LB alongside Bush (and eventual return of T.J. Edwards). At $3.75M AAV, this is excellent value. Alongside Bush, this pair begins to answer the LB hole created by Edmunds' release, but depth remains thin with Edwards and Sewell injured.
Re-signed on 2yr/$5.5M (max $8M). Keenum is a player-coach hybrid and veteran resource for Caleb Williams. QB is not a need — this is purely roster maintenance behind Williams.

Re-signed on 2yr/up to $6M. Hardy led the Bears with 19 special teams tackles in 2025 and appeared in 77.9% of special teams snaps. He has value as a core special teamer and rotational D-lineman but is not a starting EDGE. Retention keeps the depth chain intact but doesn't address the starter need at EDGE.
Re-signed on 1-year deal. McFadden was an emergency starter (guard) in the NFC Divisional Round. Pure depth/swing IOL. No starter-level impact.
Re-signed on 1-year minimum-level deal. Hicks is depth/special teams behind Coby Bryant. Does not solve the S2 starter need.

Re-signed on a 1yr/$1M deal. Benedet entered the starting LT competition in 2025 and has upside. At minimum cost, this is a camp body / developmental hold who may win the LT competition — but signing him doesn't change the team's draft priority at OT.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: EDGE escalates to the Bears' No. 1 draft need. Chicago ranked 31st in pass-rush win rate and tied for 5th-fewest sacks (35) in 2025. They missed on all top free agent EDGE targets (Phillips, Hendrickson, Oweh all signed elsewhere at elite AAV). Montez Sweat is the only proven starter. Dayo Odeyingbo is recovering from an Achilles. Robinson departed. Street is depth-only. GM Poles confirmed EDGE is their biggest need and they expect the draft to provide the answer. Must target with pick No. 25.

Bryant signing (3yr/$40M, $25.75M gtd) fills S1 with a legitimate starter. However, three starting safeties departed (Byard, Brisker, Gardner-Johnson) and Bryant fills only one of those holes. S2 is completely open. Only Elijah Hicks remains as depth. Need drops from critical (tier 1) to serious (tier 2) — one starter acquired, one still needed, likely via Day 2 draft pick or later free agency.

Bush (3yr/$30M, $21M gtd) and Jackson re-sign (2yr/$7.5M) partially restore the room after Edmunds' release. Bush is a legitimate starter investment. However, T.J. Edwards is recovering from a fractured fibula and Noah Sewell has a torn Achilles — LB depth is dangerously thin. The starter duo of Bush + Edwards (when healthy) is solid, but behind them is near-empty. LB remains a tier 2 need for depth via mid-round draft picks.

Gallimore signing (2yr/$12M) adds a rotational piece to replace Billings, but he profiles as a No. 3 DT, not a true starter upgrade. Grady Jarrett (33, declining) and Gervon Dexter are the other DTs under contract. No high-quality starting DT has been added. Run defense was bottom-5 in 2025. IDL remains a priority draft target — tier 2 need, potentially Day 1 or early Day 2 selection.

Ozzy Trapilo (starter) is out most or all of 2026 with a patellar tendon injury. Bears signed Jones (1yr/$5M re-sign), Wills (1yr/$3M lottery ticket), and retained Benedet (1yr/$1M) — all one-year deals with no real commitment. None of these moves constitute a true starter investment. LT competition will be a camp storyline. This remains a legitimate Day 1 or early Day 2 draft need. Bears must find their LT of the future in this draft.

DJ Moore trade and Zaccheaus departure leave Rome Odunze as the clear WR1, with Luther Burden III and Jahdae Walker as developing WR2/3 options. Kalif Raymond (1yr/$5.1M) adds only a WR4/returner. The group has youth upside but lacks a proven WR2 complement to Odunze. Bears need a mid-round WR with separation ability or a scheme-fit who can grow into the WR2 role. Tier rises from 3 to 2.

Garrett Bradbury trade acquisition addresses the immediate hole created by Dalman's retirement. Bradbury is a proven starting center familiar to Ben Johnson's system. However, this is a 1-year deal at $5.7M — no long-term commitment. Joe Thuney and Jonah Jackson anchor the guard spots. Bears should still draft a developmental center in mid-rounds to have a long-term answer, but the immediate crisis is resolved. Drops from tier 1 emergency to tier 3 depth consideration.

Nahshon Wright's departure (Pro Bowl, 5 INTs in 2025) creates a real hole at CB2/nickel. Jaylon Johnson is a locked-in CB1. Cam Lewis (2yr/$6M) is depth-only — a reserve with 10 career starts. The Bears re-signed Jaylon Jones (RFA) to hold the slot. CB2 role is unsettled. Should target a CB in rounds 2-4 who can develop into a starter. Rises from tier 3 to tier 2.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion safety (4 INTs in 2025) fills critical void as Bears' new S1 on a genuine 3-year commitment
NFL's only player with multiple pick-sixes in 2025 replaces Tremaine Edmunds as Chicago's starting linebacker
Familiar to Ben Johnson's system, Bradbury is the immediate bridge starter at center after Drew Dalman's stunning retirement
Career-high 3.5 sacks in 2025 makes Gallimore the rotational DT tasked with replacing Andrew Billings at nose tackle
Ben Johnson reunion gives Chicago a proven WR4/returner to replace Devin Duvernay after DJ Moore's departure
6-year Bills veteran adds CB depth after Nahshon Wright's departure but projects as a reserve, not a starter

key_losses.headline: Bears trade WR1 Moore for draft capital, pivoting to Odunze/Burden as the receiving core enters Year 2
Pro Bowl center's shocking retirement at 27 forced an emergency trade for Bradbury and leaves Chicago without a long-term C answer
Released to create $15M in cap space, Edmunds led the Bears in tackles in 2025 — replaced in theory by Devin Bush
NFL's 2025 interceptions leader (7 INTs) leaves Chicago, deepening an already depleted safety room
Multi-year starting safety departs on a prove-it deal, leaving Coby Bryant without a proven partner at S2
Pro Bowl breakthrough (5 INTs, 11 PDs in 2025) walks in free agency, creating an unexpected CB2 need
